content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 3, 2017

Jesse W. Barton
Gallery & Barton
A Professional Law Corporation
1112 I Street, Suite 240
Sacramento, CA 95814-2865

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-002

Dear Mr. Barton:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions under the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Tuolumne County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Do the conflict of interest provisions for either the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Ron Kopf, a member of the Board of Directors for the Tuolumne Utilities District ("TUD"), from participating in any Board vote involving the Yosemite Junction project?

CONCLUSION

No. As explained below, neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibits Director Kopf from voting on the Yosemite Junction project.?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-002
Page No. 2

FACTS

You represent TUD, a county water district located in Tuolumne County. TUD is governed
by a board of five directors. Director Kopf is a longtime County resident with significant County
business ties and interests. You have been authorized by Director Kopf to seek advice on his behalf.

Director Kopf is the sole owner and operator of a consulting business, RDK Development,
in the County. His services include assisting business owners and developers obtain various permits
and other entitlements necessary to develop property within the County. The gross income of the
consulting business over the past year was in excess of $100,000.

TUD is the largest water and sewer provider in the County. It provides water and sewer
service based upon established fees, charges, and rates that are set in its Water Rules and
Regulations as well as its Sewer Ordinance. When a large development project is proposed (i.e., one
that requires new infrastructure or upgrades), TUD requires the project proponent to work out an
agreement through which the type, timing and extent of the various improvements are agreed upon
("Water and Sewer Agreement").

The costs associated with TUD connecting to these newly installed or upgraded facilities are
fixed by the TUD Rules and Regulations or Sewer Ordinance and these costs are included in the
Water and Sewer Agreement. They are referred to as Connection Fees and Capacity Charges. Once
the improvements are completed, the Connection Fees and Capacity Charges are paid by the
developer, and TUD connects to the improved system. Water and sewer is provided to
the new development on the same terms that TUD provides to other customers.

There is one project in particular, Yosemite Junction, which is expected to come before the
Board in 2017. The Yosemite Junction project calls for an 18-hole public golf course, 120-room
hotel, 300 vacation condos, 50 single-family lots and 20 low-income apartments. Mr. Troy Claverin
is the project manager for the Yosemite Junction project. Neither RDK Development nor Director
Kopf has performed any planning or consulting work on the Yosemite Junction project. Neither
RDK Development nor Mr. Kopf has given or received any compensation from the Yosemite
Junction Project.

However, Director Kopf is working with Mr. Claverin on a separate project in Calaveras
County. At this stage, it is merely a potential project to develop lands in Calaveras County because
this project will first require Calaveras County to adopt an updated General Plan. The updated
General Plan will need to add a "mixed use" land use designation to the Calaveras County Code. As
of yet, Calaveras County has not added the needed new land use designation. If Calaveras County
does add such new land use designation, then Director Kopf and Mr. Claverin will be partners in the
project and will have to secure funding for the project, design the project, have the project approved
by Calaveras County, and then build the project. If Calaveras County does not add the needed land
use designation, the project as currently envisioned will not be built.

Although Mr. Claverin has signed an agreement with the landowner in Calaveras County to
purchase the property necessary for the potential project, Mr. Claverin does not yet own the

File No. A-17-002
Page No. 3

property. The agreement only obligates Mr. Claverin to raise the necessary funds to purchase the
property in a certain amount of time. If the funds are not raised, the agreement is void. Neither RDK
Development nor Director Kopf has invested any money in the Calaveras County project. Neither
RDK Development nor Director Kopf has received any money from Mr. Claverin for the Calaveras
County project. Director Kopf's involvement has been limited to writing letters and speaking on the
project's behalf at the Calaveras County Planning Commission meetings to advocate for the new
land use designation.

Mr. Kopf has no right to any future compensation from the Calaveras County project and
has no continuing obligation to participate in the project. Director Kopf could walk away from the
project at any time. The success (or failure) of the Yosemite Junction project does not make the
Calaveras County project more or less likely to come to fruition.

ANALYSIS

Conflicts Under the Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state
or local government agency, including city councilmembers. Financial interests from which
conflicts of interests may arise are defined in Section 87103 as follows:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)) or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).) An indirect interest in business or real property means a business or
real property interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of the official.

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) "Income" is
defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share
of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns
directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).)

• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $470 or more within
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

Based on your facts, Director Kopf has an interest in RDK Development as a business entity
and a source of income. In addition, the clients of RDK would be considered a source of income to

File No. A-17-002
Page No. 4

Director Kopf to the extent that their income to RDK totals $500 or more within 12 months prior to
the decision.²

The issue then distills to whether the decision involving the Yosemite Junction Project will
have a foreseeable and material financial effect on RDK.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party
in, or subject of, a governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Your interest is not explicitly
involved in the governmental decision at issue. For interests that are not explicitly involved, as here,
the effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. "In general, if the financial
effect can be recognized its a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is
reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 18701(b).)

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards for a decision's effect on an
official's interest in a source of income. Director Kopf has an interest in RDK as sources of income.
And Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that the materiality of a decision's effect on a business
entity that is a source of income to the official is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1.
Regulation 18702.1(b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's effect on an
official's interest in a business entity that is not, as here, explicitly involved in the decision.
Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that such an effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient
information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the
value of the business entity.³

As mentioned, the Yosemite Junction project, which will likely come before the Board in
2017, calls for an 18-hole public golf course, 120-room hotel, 300 vacation condos, 50 single-
family lots and 20 low-income apartments. RDK Development has neither performed any planning
or consulting work nor received any compensation for the project. There are simply no facts to
suggest that the project will contribute in any way to a change in the value of RDK. And despite the
fact that Mr. Claverin, a potential partner in a separate and future development project, is the project
manager of the Yosemite Junction project, there are no facts showing that Director Kopf has a
present financial interest in Mr. Claverin, or any business he is associated with, that might affect our
analysis.⁴

Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit Director Kopf from taking part in
the decision concerning the Yosemite Junction project.

² Under Section 82030(a), if a public official owns a 10-percent or greater interest in a business, a source of
income to that business is also a source of income to the official. We note that nothing in your facts suggest Director
Kopf has an interest in Mr. Claverin, or any company he is associated with, as a source of income.

³ Regulation 18702.1(b) also provides examples of decisions that may have a material effect on a business
entity.

⁴ We urge you to request further written advice from the Commission should this change prior to the vote on
the Yosemite Junction project.

File No. A-17-002
Page No. 5


Conflicts Under Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.)

Here, you have provided no facts to suggest the vote on the Yosemite Junction project involves a contract. Section 1090 therefore has no application to this decision. And, even if a contract were at issue, you have provided no facts to show that Director Kopf would have a financial interest in it.

Accordingly, based on these facts, Section 1090 does not prohibit Director Kopf from voting on the Yosemite Junction project.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:jgl

in any Board vote involving the Yosemite Junction project?

voting on the Yosemite Junction project?

sections.analysis:
Conflicts U11der the Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state 
or local government agency, including city councilmembers. Financial interests from which

$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a}} or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d}.} 
• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).} An indirect interest in business or real property means a business or 
real property interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of the official. 
• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) "Income" is 
defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share 
of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns 
directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a}.) 
• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $470 or more within 
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e}.) 
• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.}

and a source of income. In addition, the clients of RDK would be considered a source of income to  
Director Kopf to the extent that their income to RDK totals $500 or more within 12 months prior to 
the decision.2

Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit Director Kopf from taking part in

Conflicts Under Section 1090

member of the Board of Directors for the Tuolumne Utilities District ("TUD"), from participating 
in any Board vote involving the Yosemite Junction project?

CONCLUSION: No. As explained below, neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibits Director Kopf from 
voting on the Yosemite Junction project?
